ecosystemBackend.name: bapX ecosystem backend
ecosystemBackend.dataStore: JSON files with documented JSON Schema
ecosystemBackend.policy: Build the central bapX ecosystem backend as first-party Node.js/TypeScript with JSON-schema-first company, project, connector, workflow, content, billing, release, and admin records. SQL is not the product data model for agentic work. If SQL/vector internals exist later, expose them only behind generated JSON Schemas, TypeScript contracts, and agent-readable files.

blogPosts.title: agents.bapx.in becomes the active product direction
blogPosts.copy: The active product direction is now agents.bapx.in and @bapx/agent, with the legacy checkout removed from the workspace.
content: bapX is moving current product work to agents.bapx.in. The previous runtime-first framing created confusion between an install path and the command center we need to ship.

The active repo is /root/bapx.in/project-packages-git/agents and the package direction is @bapx/agent. The public UI should point users to connectors, company workspaces, hosted sandboxes, SSH targets, Vercel, webhooks, cron, and API/MCP records instead of asking them to install dependencies inside a server first.
This release note exists so agents and humans can read the product state from the blog surface, not from scattered chat memory.

blogPosts.title: JSON-schema-first records for agentic company workspaces
blogPosts.copy: Why bapX company, project, connector, workflow, release, billing, and content records should be JSON-schema-first before any storage implementation.
content: Agentic systems need data that can be inspected, diffed, edited, and moved by both humans and agents. For bapX, that means company records, project records, connector records, workflow records, release records, billing state, and blog content should start as documented JSON with generated TypeScript contracts.

SQL can exist later as an internal implementation detail, but it should not be the product model agents have to guess. The agent-readable contract is the JSON Schema and the OKF-linked markdown around it.
admin.bapx.in should make these records visible: what exists, what changed, what repo shipped it, what issue/PR validated it, and what public surface now depends on it.

blogPosts.title: Open source projects in the bapX research lane
blogPosts.copy: Public repositories including pak-it, map.mmd, x8Dsub-byte, and ByteOmniDiffus-RLM-HyperAGI, with contribution paths through GitHub.
content: Open source content should be driven from the blog category so each project can carry context, release notes, contribution notes, and SEO text without hardcoding separate pages.
Each project card links to GitHub, but the blog explains why the project exists, how it relates to bapX research, and what contributors or agents should read first.
The current open source lane includes pak-it, map.mmd, x8Dsub-byte, and ByteOmniDiffus-RLM-HyperAGI.

blogPosts.title: Media Hub content belongs in company posts
blogPosts.copy: Media Hub is company/client/service content. It should be updated through company blog posts and reflected on mediahub.bapx.in.
content: mediahub.bapx.in is the client portfolio and company service surface. The durable source of new updates should be blog content in the Company category, then rendered into Media Hub sections where useful.
This keeps client updates, service notes, research-led business content, and public announcements in one publishing flow.
The admin publishing workflow should track which posts feed Media Hub and which posts are only company notes.

blogPosts.title: Repository maps as a working knowledge graph
blogPosts.copy: How repo maps connect packages, routes, schemas, MCP tools, docs, connectors, providers, and architecture gaps.
content: A repo map is not decoration. It is a working knowledge graph for agents and humans before they edit a repo.

The map should show hosts, routed pages, JSON data, schemas, products, OAuth providers, blog/docs sections, Media Hub clients, open source projects, admin panels, workspace roots, billing, API/MCP gateways, and known gaps.
Every meaningful change should regenerate the map so the next agent starts from current structure.
